teamObjective.explanation: My hand is very weak (3-6 of clubs, no trumps), so I cannot realistically win the trick myself. I want to play the smallest card to let my partner, who acts after both opponents, potentially take control with a higher card or trump.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner likely holds some higher cards or trumps since they have three cards and no public plays have occurred. They may be able to beat a low club lead, but this is uncertain because their exact hand is hidden.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponent player 1 has only two cards and is near finishing, so they may be holding a high card or trump to try to win and go out. Opponent player 3 has three cards and may also hold cards that beat a low club, but no specific cards are known.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of clubs is the smallest legal lead. It sheds my weakest card, minimizes the chance I accidentally feed a just-higher card to an opponent, and gives my partner the best chance to overtake with a higher card or trump after both opponents have played.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 6 is the highest card in my hand but still very low and non-trump. Playing it now would remove my best remaining card without improving the chance of winning the trick, and it could be beaten by the same opponent cards anyway.
The 4 is slightly higher than the 3 but otherwise identical in effect. Since all my cards are weak clubs, I should keep marginally higher cards for later and discard the lowest one first.
Same reasoning as the 4 and 6: any single club lead is likely to be beaten, so the smallest lead is the least costly and preserves better low cards for future turns.

risks: An opponent may have a trump or higher non-trump card and win the trick.
Opponent player 1, with only two cards, could win and go out if they hold a strong finishing card.
My partner may not be able to beat the opponents' responses, leaving opponents in control.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Play the minimum-ranked card (3 of clubs) to reduce the value given away and keep my slightly higher clubs for later plays, while hoping partner can take the trick.
